planting.note: Plant corms 2–4 inches deep in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; Jack-in-the-Pulpit is one of the most distinctive native woodland wildflowers of eastern North America, with a unique hooded spathe and striking red berry cluster in fall that makes it instantly recognizable and irreplaceable in native woodland gardens.

directions: Plant corms 2–4 inches deep in fall or early spring in consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; Jack-in-the-Pulpit does not tolerate dry conditions and will decline without consistent moisture through the growing season. Arisaema triphyllum is one of eastern North America's most distinctive and beloved woodland wildflowers, producing a single hooded spathe (the 'pulpit') striped in green and maroon-purple enclosing a club-shaped spadix (the 'Jack') from which the tiny flowers emerge in spring; the corm is dioecious or changes sex based on its size, with smaller corms producing male flowers and larger ones producing female flowers that develop into a compact cluster of brilliant scarlet-red berries (extremely toxic) that persists into fall after the leaves die back. The berries are the most ornamentally striking element. All parts are caustic and toxic if consumed raw. An iconic native spring ephemeral for moist woodland gardens; companion to trilliums, bloodroot, and ferns.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Polemonium reptans is a native woodland species distinct from the more commonly grown P. caeruleum, with more deeply shade-tolerant constitution and a looser, more delicate growth habit suited to naturalized woodland gardens.

directions: Plant in spring or fall in consistently moist, humus-enriched, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; Polemonium reptans tolerates more shade than the European P. caeruleum and is well-suited to naturalized plantings under deciduous trees alongside other spring wildflowers. Polemonium reptans is a native eastern North American woodland wildflower of moist, shaded stream banks, forest floors, and ravines, producing delicate, finely pinnate, ladder-like leaves (the 'Jacob's Ladder' pattern from which the genus takes its name) and clusters of nodding, sky-blue to lavender-blue, five-petaled flowers in April and May that are delicate and beautiful in a woodland setting. It goes dormant by midsummer in heat and drought, making it a spring ephemeral in most of its range; companion it with ferns and hostas that fill the space after it dies back. The cultivar 'Stairway to Heaven' with white-and-green variegated leaves is popular for brightening shade. An underused native for shade gardens in zones 4–8.

planting.note: Plant small corms (clusters of rice-grain-sized cormlets) 2–3 inches deep in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Dutchman's Breeches is a spring ephemeral that disappears by early summer — plant it where summer perennials or ferns will fill the void it leaves behind.

directions: Plant cormlets (clusters of small, rice-grain-like corms) 2–3 inches deep in fall in moist, humus-rich woodland soil in partial to full shade; in spring the delicate foliage emerges early and the flowers appear, then the entire plant disappears underground by early June. Dutchman's Breeches (Dicentra cucullaria) is one of the most charming and distinctive of all native spring ephemerals — small, pale-green, finely divided leaves emerge in earliest spring alongside arching stems bearing 4–10 waxy, white, heart-shaped flowers with two inflated spurs that form a distinctive pantaloon or 'breeches' shape pointing upward, as if a tiny pair of trousers hung to dry on a line. The flowers are one of the first spring forest wildflowers and are pollinated primarily by long-tongued bumblebee queens that can reach the nectar at the tips of the spurs. By June the plant is dormant and invisible underground. Outstanding companion to Virginia bluebells, trout lily, bloodroot, and other spring ephemerals in moist woodland gardens; all parts toxic if eaten.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or sow fresh seed in fall; Twinleaf is a charming and underused native woodland plant of the barberry family named in honor of Thomas Jefferson by its discoverer William Bartram, producing distinctive deeply two-lobed leaves that resemble a folded butterfly wing and ephemeral white flowers in early spring.

directions: Plant in spring in deeply humus-rich, consistently moist, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; Twinleaf is slow to establish but long-lived and gradually expands into a colony through root divisions. Jeffersonia diphylla (Twinleaf) is a native eastern North American woodland plant of the barberry family named by the American botanist Benjamin Smith Barton in honor of Thomas Jefferson in 1792 for Jefferson's wide-ranging botanical and scientific curiosity; the common name 'twinleaf' refers to the distinctive paired, deeply bilobed leaves that appear to consist of two separate mirror-image halves folded together along a central crease — unlike any other North American wildflower leaf. In early spring (March–April) the plant produces solitary, eight-petaled, pure white flowers on bare stems before the leaves fully expand; the flowers last only a day or two. After flowering the leaves expand to their full size and remain attractive through summer. An unusual and botanically interesting native wildflower for moist woodland gardens; relatively rare in cultivation.

common_name: Turk's Cap (Turk's Cap Mallow, Wax Mallow)

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants or rooted cuttings in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Turk's Cap is outstanding in partial to full shade where it is one of the most shade-tolerant flowering native shrubs for the Gulf Coast and Southeast, blooming reliably in conditions that defeat most flowering shrubs.

directions: Plant in spring in well-drained, fertile to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to deep shade; Turk's Cap is one of the most heat- and drought-tolerant native shrubs of the Gulf Coast and is also one of the most shade-tolerant flowering plants in the region. Malvaviscus arboreus var. drummondii (Turk's Cap) is a native perennial shrub of the Gulf Coast and Texas lowlands from Louisiana to Mexico, producing distinctive, red, partially-opened hibiscus-like flowers with petals that never fully unfurl (remaining curled around the prominent staminal column in the distinctive 'turban' or 'fez' shape that gives it the common name). The tubular, nectar-rich flowers are among the most important hummingbird food sources in Gulf Coast and Texas gardens from summer through late fall; native bees and butterflies also visit. Edible small red fruits follow the flowers and are eaten by mockingbirds and other wildlife. Dies to the ground in freezes north of zone 9 but re-sprouts from the root crown reliably. Outstanding for shaded gardens, natural areas, and wildlife plantings in zones 7–11.
